nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, seagrass meadows and sandy/rubble areas, typically out in the open across exposed substrate during the day.

decorPreferences: Deep, well-established sand bed for burrowing and sand-sifting
Open sand flats with access to detritus and biofilm
Mature live rock to seed the sand bed with microfauna
Inlet/outlet covers and grates over powerheads to prevent contact with impellers

diet.notes: In the wild, ingests large amounts of sand and sifts out detritus, biofilms, microalgae and tiny organic particles, expelling the cleaned sand behind it. In captivity it is generally considered self-feeding on a sufficiently large, mature sand bed; supplemental feeding is rarely necessary and overstocked or undernourished individuals tend to slowly starve. Sources occasionally describe the species as omnivorous, but its functional role in the aquarium is that of a sand-sifting detritivore.

avoidWith: Powerheads without intake guards (mechanical injury can trigger toxin release)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 5992x3995. Tack-sharp, well-lit whole-animal in-situ shot on a sand flat; clearly shows the diagnostic sand-coated body with jet-black cleared patches. Best overall.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x912. In-situ on reef rubble; classic grey sand-coating with black patches, whole animal in frame. Good secondary angle.
Tim Parkinson; 2304x1728. Two individuals in shallow clear water showing the distinctive black spotting; adds context/variety.

summary: The Black Sea Cucumber (Holothuria atra) is a large sand-sifting invertebrate from the Indo-Pacific that processes detritus from mature sand beds. Generally considered reef-safe, it can release potent toxins called holothurin if injured or killed, posing a serious risk to tank inhabitants.
careNotes:
The Black Sea Cucumber is a sand-sifting detritivore from the tropical Indo-Pacific — ranging from the Red Sea through Indonesia to Hawaii — that processes detritus, biofilm, and microalgae out of the sand bed. A deep (5 cm or more), mature, biologically active sand bed is essentially a requirement: undersized or sterile sand beds lead to slow starvation, which is the primary long-term mortality risk in captivity. Suitable water conditions are a temperature of 22–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, and salinity at SG 1.023–1.025. The species is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward corals and other tank inhabitants under normal conditions.

The most important husbandry consideration is the risk posed by holothurin, a potent toxin this species can release when injured, severely stressed, or dying undetected in the tank. In some accounts, stressed individuals also eviscerate — ejecting internal organs as a defense response. Either event can introduce enough toxin into the water to kill fish across an otherwise stable system. To reduce this risk: cover all powerhead intakes and overflow weirs so the animal cannot be drawn into impellers; avoid housing it with species known to nip at echinoderms, such as pufferfish, triggerfish, large angelfish, harlequin shrimp, and aggressive crabs; and keep activated carbon and the capacity for large water changes on hand so the system can be treated quickly if a toxin event is suspected. The species is also very sensitive to copper-based medications and to elevated nitrate levels, both of which should be kept firmly in check. In the aquarium hobby, the Black Sea Cucumber is less commonly encountered than the related Tigertail Sea Cucumber (Holothuria edulis), but care requirements for the two are broadly similar.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria in any meaningful way. Wild reproduction is by broadcast spawning (primarily in summer and autumn) and, distinctively, by asexual transverse fission — the animal constricts and splits into two short individuals that regenerate the missing end. Asexual fission has been observed in captivity but is not a reliable reproduction route for hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade-source care reference. Lists Care Level: Expert Only, Diet: Omnivore, Temperament: Peaceful, Reef Safe: Yes, Temp 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, sensitive to copper and high nitrate. Tank-size guidance is given as a stocking ratio (3 in of cucumber per 20 gal) rather than an absolute minimum; cross-checked against AquariumDomain's 125 gal figure for the adult-tank floor.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Assigned secondary source did not return a dedicated species page for Holothuria atra on fetch; swapped to additional secondaries below to meet the cross-verification minimum.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Black Sea Cucumber Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025), peaceful temperament, reef-compatible, expert care level, and an explicit minimum tank size of 125 US gal (~473 L) with adult size around 24 in / 60 cm. Used as the basis for biasing tank size up to 450 L.

additional.name: Reeflex — Holothuria (Halodeima) atra
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20–60 cm), wild depth range (0–30 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, diet (algae/detritus/plankton/sediment feeder), and reinforced the holothurin toxicity warning with documented cases of complete fish die-offs after stress events.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Holothuria atra
additional.notes: Taxonomy reference (Holothuriidae, Holothuriida, subgenus Halodeima) and confirmation of native range, ~20 cm typical / up to 60 cm size, asexual reproduction by transverse fission, and the red-fluid toxin defense (steroidal sapogenins / triterpene glycosides).

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Capable of releasing potent saponin toxins (holothurin) if injured, drawn into a powerhead, or dying — documented to wipe out fish stocks in otherwise stable tanks. Also reaches up to about 60 cm and requires a large, mature sand bed for long-term survival, so it is best left to experienced reef-keepers with appropriately sized, fish-safe systems.
